g.text: 1. Client apps should be able to send a notification through a service to iOS, Android, email and SMS channels
2. Notification service should respect user preferences for which channel the notification gets sent to. Default
settings are to send notifications to all channels
1. System should be fault tolerant - we should try not to send duplicate messages
2. Scale up to 10 million notifications a day
-notificationPreferences (json blob of preferences, ex: iOs: True, Android: False etc)
SQS allows a message to be scheduled for delivery if desired,
otherwise message will be pushed immediately to a worker.
1. Client app sends notification to Notification Service
2. Notification looks up user preferences and creates
a new message to be sent to corresponding queues. A
record of a new notification is created in the database
3. Worker takes message from queue and sends the
message over to the 3rd Party Channel Provider,
(APN, FCM, Twilio, Email Service). Worker updates
notification record with a status of success.
4. Customer receives message through relevant channel
Deep Dive 1 - How do we prevent duplicate notifications from coming through?
In case of erroneous reprocessing of notifications from the queue,
the worker can look up the notification by id to see if it already
exists and if the status is successful. If the status is a success,
then we will not send the message off to the 3rd Party Channel Provider.
Deep Dive 2 - How do we scale up to 10 million messages per day?
We can autoscale the Notification Service based on CPU utilization,
and do the same for our workers. If each notification was 200 bytes
and we have 10 million messages per day, it would take up
2 GB daily -> about 1 TB annually. Older notifications could be sent to
cold storage after a certain period of time.
Deep Dive 3 - How do we ensure that messages are delivered within 1s
We can ensure that workers are autoscaled to serve the traffic load
based on SQS queue size or CPU utilization.
We could also employ a Template Service for emails so that the message
can be quickly created once the worker has the message from SQS.
Deep Dive 4 - How does the worker handle errors if the 3rd party provider
is not responding for any reason?
If there is a visible error, we update the number of attempts for that notification
and put the message back onto the queue. For invisible failures, SQS has a visibility
timeout. If the worker doesn't process the message without the visibility timeout,
then the message will get processed by another worker.
We could also employ circuit breakers before the worker communicates with the APN,
TCM, Twilio or Email Service. If there are more than a certain number of failures over
a threshold, then we can fail quickly and alert on the failures.
